foundingEvent.title: The Founding Charter
foundingEvent.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy
foundingEvent.brief: Your city is young and growing fast
foundingEvent.caseFact: A city's "metabolism" can be linear (resources flow in, waste flows out and is discarded) or circular (waste is fed back as a resource). The waste hierarchy ranks strategies from most preferred (reduce) to least (landfill).

entry.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy

actions.label: "Dispose and Move On" — disposal-led.
actions.resultExplain: The cheapest, simplest start — collect and bury. You've chosen a **linear metabolism**: easy now, but land fills up and pollution accumulates, so disposal/crisis events will dominate your future.

actions.label: "Burn for Power" — thermal/energy-led.
actions.resultExplain: Reduce waste volume and make electricity. A partly-circular thermal path — capacity and energy upside, but emissions, ash and NIMBY politics will recur.

actions.label: "Recover and Recycle" — recovery-led.
actions.resultExplain: Treat waste as a resource. A **circular metabolism** built on sorting and markets — higher cost and participation-dependent, but it compounds into resilience and recycling income.

actions.label: "Reduce at Source" — prevention-first.
actions.resultExplain: Stop waste before it exists — the **top of the waste hierarchy**. The most sustainable and most expensive/slow start; unlocks zero-waste opportunities but strains the early budget and tests public patience.

justify.question: Why does this founding choice matter so much for sustainability?
options: It sets whether the city runs a linear (discard) or circular (resource) metabolism, shaping all later outcomes

roundEvents.title: The Convenience Trap
roundEvents.brief: Retailers are wrapping everything in single-use plastic to attract shoppers; bin volumes jump overnight
roundEvents.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Levy a packaging charge.
actions.resultExplain: Cutting waste at source is top of the hierarchy, but businesses and shoppers resent paying now; the payoff comes later.

actions.label: Run a "bring-your-own" campaign.
actions.resultExplain: Voluntary behaviour change is cheap and popular but slow and partial.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect it.
actions.resultExplain: Easiest path — but you've locked in linear metabolism and rising waste.

justify.question: Why is reducing packaging "better" than recycling it?
options: It prevents the waste and its disposal cost from ever existing

roundEvents.title: Built to be Binned
roundEvents.brief: Cheap goods designed to be replaced not repaired are flooding the city
roundEvents.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair-and-reuse hubs.
actions.resultExplain: Reuse extends product life and builds a repair culture, but depends on citizen uptake.

actions.label: Right-to-repair regulation.
actions.resultExplain: Forces durability upstream; effective but lobbied against by manufacturers.

actions.label: Expand collection for the extra bulk.
actions.resultExplain: Treats the symptom, not the cause — waste keeps climbing.

justify.question: What does "built-in obsolescence" do to a city's waste stream?
options: Continuously enlarges it as products are replaced not repaired

roundEvents.title: Lockdown Leftovers
roundEvents.brief: A boom in online food delivery buries the city in takeaway containers
roundEvents.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Mandate reusable container scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the loop on packaging, but needs return logistics and buy-in.

actions.label: Add recycling points at hawker centres.
actions.resultExplain: Captures material if residents sort correctly.

actions.label: Absorb it into landfill.
actions.resultExplain: Quick relief, but plastic is non-biodegradable and piles up.

justify.question: Why did delivery culture raise the footprint so fast?
options: More packaging per meal, mostly non-recyclable plastic

roundEvents.title: Edge City
roundEvents.brief: A new suburb sprawls at the city fringe with no waste collection yet
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Extend integrated collection + sorting.
actions.resultExplain: Brings the suburb into a circular system from day one — costly but future-proof.

actions.label: Basic collection only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper, but you've designed in linear habits.

actions.label: Let residents self-manage.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money but invites fly-tipping and resentment.

justify.question: Why design waste systems before a district is built?
options: Retrofitting habits and infrastructure later is far harder and costlier
It is required by law everywhere

roundEvents.title: Streets of Refuse
roundEvents.brief: Collection can't keep up; rubbish accumulates on streets and catches fire
roundEvents.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in collection fleet + routes.
actions.resultExplain: Solves the immediate health risk; doesn't yet address disposal.

actions.label: Partner with informal collectors.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap, boosts circularity and livelihoods — leverages existing networks.

actions.label: Ignore it for now.
actions.resultExplain: Free now, but disease vectors and distrust accumulate.

justify.question: Why is uncollected waste a health emergency, not just an eyesore?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water

roundEvents.title: Sort It Out
roundEvents.brief: You can launch a citywide source-separation pilot now
roundEvents.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full curbside source separation.
actions.resultExplain: Big circularity gain, but expensive and reliant on participation.

actions.label: Drop-off centres only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper, lower capture — convenient for some.

actions.label: Delay until budget improves.
actions.resultExplain: Saves cash; you forgo early circular momentum.

justify.question: Why does source separation only work with citizen participation?
options: Contaminated mixed waste can't be recycled efficiently

roundEvents.title: Factory Lines
roundEvents.brief: A new industrial park brings jobs — and commercial/industrial waste beyond domestic rubbish
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require industrial waste audits + reuse.
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry toward technical-cycle reuse; some firms grumble.

actions.label: Charge firms a disposal fee.
actions.resultExplain: Raises revenue and mild incentive, but firms may cut corners.

actions.label: Accept the waste into general disposal.
actions.resultExplain: Welcomes business but loads your disposal system.

justify.question: Why must cities plan for more than domestic rubbish?
options: Industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied waste streams

roundEvents.title: Wrap and Throw
roundEvents.brief: With sewers lacking, residents wrap waste and throw it into the river
roundEvents.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation + collection.
actions.resultExplain: Tackles root cause; expensive but protects water and health.

actions.label: River clean-up + awareness.
actions.resultExplain: Visible win and behaviour nudge, but waste still needs somewhere to go.

actions.label: Tolerate it.
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now; contaminates wells and waterways downstream.

justify.question: Why does dumping in rivers harm areas far away?
options: Waste and leachate travel downstream and to the sea, spreading contamination

roundEvents.title: Teach the Future
roundEvents.brief: Schools want a waste-literacy programme to shape habits early
roundEvents.caseFact: Changing public attitudes — linking waste to climate and making sorting logical — underpins Copenhagen's success.

actions.label: Citywide schools programme.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap long-term participation builder; slow to show in metrics.

actions.label: One-off media campaign.
actions.resultExplain: A small nudge with limited reach.

actions.label: Skip it.
actions.resultExplain: No cost, no cultural shift toward circularity.

justify.question: Why invest in attitudes, not just bins?

roundEvents.title: Digital Discards
roundEvents.brief: Rising phone and gadget turnover creates a stream of hazardous electronic waste
roundEvents.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back + urban mining.
actions.resultExplain: Recovers critical materials and prevents toxics leaking — "urban mining."

actions.label: Hazardous-waste drop-off only.
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination; recovers less value.

actions.label: Mix it into general waste.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but leaches heavy metals and wastes critical materials.

justify.question: Why treat e-waste as a resource, not rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and toxic substances that must be contained

roundEvents.title: The Last Cell
roundEvents.brief: Your main landfill will hit capacity within the year
roundEvents.caseFact: Cities are running out of landfill space; finding suitable land near dense urban areas is increasingly hard.

actions.label: Build an engineered offshore/lined landfill.
actions.resultExplain: Buys safe capacity (Semakau-style lining) but it's finite and costly.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump.
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief, severe long-term health and fire risk.

actions.label: Slash inflow via recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Attacks the cause, not the hole — slower but sustainable.

justify.question: Why is "just build another dump" a trap?
options: Land is finite and dumps create lasting health

roundEvents.title: The Cheap Option
roundEvents.brief: Budget hawks push for the cheapest possible disposal
roundEvents.caseFact: Over 90% of waste in low-income countries is openly dumped or burned, with serious consequences.

actions.label: Resist; fund engineered disposal.
actions.resultExplain: Protects health, strains budget — the responsible call.

actions.label: Approve the open dump.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money now; mortgages public health later.

actions.label: Hybrid: controlled tipping with cover.
actions.resultExplain: A middle path — better than open dumping, worse than full engineering.

justify.question: What's the core difference between a dump and an engineered landfill?
options: Lining, leachate treatment and controls that limit contamination

roundEvents.title: Island of Last Resort
roundEvents.brief: Engineers propose a lined offshore landfill with a leachate treatment plant
roundEvents.caseFact: Semakau (1999, SGD$610m) is lined with membrane, marine clay and rock; leachate is treated to keep waters clean.

actions.label: Fund it fully.
actions.resultExplain: Best-in-class containment, but heavy upfront debt.

actions.label: Cheaper unlined version.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money but leachate threatens groundwater.

actions.label: Reject; double down on recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids the landfill entirely if you can cut volume enough.

justify.question: Why does lining/leachate treatment matter most when a landfill sits above an aquifer?
options: Leachate can contaminate groundwater used by people

roundEvents.title: Not in Our Backyard, Either
roundEvents.brief: A rural town that receives your trucked waste refuses further loads
roundEvents.caseFact: Collected waste often ends in landfills far from the city, creating "waste hinterlands" that bear the burden.

actions.label: Negotiate benefits-sharing deal.
actions.resultExplain: Fair compensation keeps the route open and respects the hinterland.

actions.label: Truck it further away.
actions.resultExplain: Solves it short-term but raises transport emissions and footprint.

actions.label: Reduce what you send.
actions.resultExplain: Shrinking the stream eases hinterland pressure for good.

justify.question: Why is exporting waste to a hinterland not a real solution?
options: It shifts the environmental burden elsewhere without reducing it

roundEvents.title: Poison in the Water
roundEvents.brief: Toxic leachate from your site is seeping toward local wells
roundEvents.caseFact: Studies link living near landfills to raised risks of birth defects from contaminated water and soil.

actions.label: Install a leachate treatment plant.
actions.resultExplain: Directly stops the contamination pathway; expensive but vital.

actions.label: Provide bottled water, delay fix.
actions.resultExplain: A stopgap that treats symptoms while pollution continues.

actions.label: Deny and monitor only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but erodes trust and worsens the health legacy.

justify.question: What is leachate?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying contaminants

roundEvents.title: The Invisible Threat
roundEvents.brief: Decomposing organics are building explosive methane pockets in the mound
roundEvents.caseFact: Landfills cause ~11% of global methane emissions; methane pockets at Deonar sparked a 3-month fire.

actions.label: Capture methane for energy.
actions.resultExplain: Turns a potent GHG into power — circular and profitable.

actions.label: Flare it off safely.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces explosion risk and converts methane to less-potent CO₂.

actions.label: Leave it.
actions.resultExplain: Free, but fire risk and powerful warming emissions remain.

justify.question: Why is landfill methane a climate priority?
options: Methane is a far more potent greenhouse gas than CO₂ over the short term
It is not produced by landfills

roundEvents.title: The Long Haul
roundEvents.brief: Hauling waste to distant sites is eating fuel budgets and adding emissions
roundEvents.caseFact: Fuel to transport waste over longer distances adds to a city's ecological footprint.

actions.label: Build a local transfer + sorting station.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts haul distance and recovers material en route.

actions.label: Optimise routes with smart logistics.
actions.resultExplain: Efficiency gains without solving the underlying volume.

actions.label: Accept rising costs.
actions.resultExplain: No upfront spend; bleeds budget and emissions each round.

justify.question: Why does hauling distance matter for sustainability?
options: Transport fuel adds emissions and cost to the waste system

roundEvents.title: Whose Land?
roundEvents.brief: Developers want your landfill buffer land for housing; residents want a park
roundEvents.caseFact: Proximity of landfills to residential areas creates land-use conflicts in dense cities.

actions.label: Convert a closed cell into a park.
actions.resultExplain: Well-capped landfills can become playing fields — a liveability win.

actions.label: Sell to developers.
actions.resultExplain: Quick revenue, but builds homes near contamination and angers residents.

actions.label: Keep as buffer.
actions.resultExplain: Safe and cautious; forgoes both revenue and amenity.

justify.question: How can old landfill land be reused safely?
options: capped sites can host parks or playing fields

roundEvents.title: Fly-Tipping
roundEvents.brief: New disposal fees push some to dump waste illegally on roadsides and lots
roundEvents.caseFact: Where formal disposal is costly or lacking, illegal dumping becomes an "inevitable" reality.

actions.label: Free drop-off + enforcement.
actions.resultExplain: Removes the incentive to dump while penalising offenders.

actions.label: Heavy fines only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap deterrent, but drives dumping underground if no easy alternative.

actions.label: Ignore it.
actions.resultExplain: Free, but blight and contamination spread.

justify.question: Why can disposal fees backfire without easy alternatives?
options: People resort to illegal dumping when legal disposal is too costly or hard

roundEvents.title: Digging Up the Past
roundEvents.brief: An old landfill could be mined to reclaim land and recover materials
roundEvents.caseFact: Reclaiming and reusing old sites extends land value and recovers recyclables (urban mining).

actions.label: Landfill mining + remediation.
actions.resultExplain: Recovers space and materials, but it's costly and disruptive.

actions.label: Cap and re-green only.
actions.resultExplain: Lower cost amenity gain without reclaiming materials.

actions.label: Leave the old site.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; land and materials stay locked away.

justify.question: What is "urban mining"?

roundEvents.title: Burn or Bury?
roundEvents.brief: A modern incinerator could slash landfill volume and generate power
roundEvents.caseFact: Incineration can cut waste volume 80–90%; Singapore's plants supply 2–3% of national electricity.

actions.label: Build a modern incinerator.
actions.resultExplain: Massive capacity + energy income, but pollution, NIMBY and heavy debt.

actions.label: Smaller waste-to-energy plant.
actions.resultExplain: Lower impact and cost; less capacity relief.

actions.label: Avoid burning; expand recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps air clean and materials in the loop, but capacity relief is slower.

justify.question: What is the central trade-off of incineration?
options: It frees land and makes energy but emits pollutants and faces NIMBY

roundEvents.title: Not Near My Home
roundEvents.brief: Residents near the proposed/active plant protest over health and property values
roundEvents.caseFact: Incinerators raise NIMBY concerns; studies link downwind living to health effects and lower property prices.

actions.label: Transparent siting + health monitoring.
actions.resultExplain: Engagement and monitoring rebuild trust; slows the project.

actions.label: Compensate affected residents.
actions.resultExplain: Buys acceptance without addressing emissions.

actions.label: Override the protest.
actions.resultExplain: Fast, but poisons public trust for every future policy.

justify.question: Why does public trust matter for waste infrastructure?
options: Without buy-in, siting and participation in future schemes get much harder

roundEvents.title: Private Power
roundEvents.brief: A private firm offers to build and run a waste-to-energy plant
roundEvents.caseFact: Edmonton's incinerator handles waste from seven London boroughs, generating 55 MW for ~24,000 homes.

actions.label: Public-private partnership.
actions.resultExplain: Shares cost and risk; you cede some control over emissions standards.

actions.label: Public build, public control.
actions.resultExplain: Full control of standards, full cost and debt.

actions.label: Decline; stay landfill+recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids burning and debt; capacity pressure persists.

justify.question: A risk of privatised waste-to-energy?

roundEvents.title: The Downwind Data
roundEvents.brief: A study finds elevated health risks downwind of your incinerator
roundEvents.caseFact: Research associated Edmonton's incinerator with downwind stillbirths far above upwind rates.

actions.label: Retrofit advanced flue-gas scrubbers.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts dioxins/particulates sharply; costly but protects health.

actions.label: Adjust operating hours/wind.
actions.resultExplain: Marginal mitigation; the core emissions remain.

actions.label: Dispute the study.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but harms residents and credibility.

justify.question: Why is incineration "waste of energy" sometimes, not "waste to energy"?
options: Emissions and health costs can outweigh the energy benefit if uncontrolled

roundEvents.title: What's Left Behind
roundEvents.brief: Incineration leaves toxic bottom and fly ash that must go somewhere
roundEvents.caseFact: Incineration converts waste to ash, flue gas and heat — ash disposal is a real challenge.

actions.label: Treat ash + reuse in construction.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the loop on residue where safe; needs careful testing.

actions.label: Send ash to a lined cell.
actions.resultExplain: Safe containment, consumes precious landfill space.

actions.label: Dump ash untreated.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest; heavy metals contaminate land and water.

justify.question: Why doesn't incineration make waste "disappear"?
options: It leaves ash and emissions that still need management

roundEvents.title: Selling the Spark
roundEvents.brief: The grid offers a contract for your plant's electricity
roundEvents.caseFact: Waste-to-energy can offset costs — Singapore's plants supply 2–3% of the grid.

actions.label: Long-term grid contract + heat reuse.
actions.resultExplain: Locks in revenue and recovers heat — best value from burning.

actions.label: Spot-market sales.
actions.resultExplain: Flexible income, exposed to price swings.

actions.label: Power your own facilities only.
actions.resultExplain: Modest savings, no external revenue.

justify.question: How does energy recovery change incineration's balance?
options: Revenue and displaced fossil energy partly offset its costs

roundEvents.title: The New Rules
roundEvents.brief: National regulators impose stricter air-quality limits
roundEvents.caseFact: Burning waste releases NOx, CO₂, SO₂, dioxins and particulates — regulation targets these.

actions.label: Upgrade to comply early.
actions.resultExplain: Future-proofs operations and protects health; upfront cost.

actions.label: Buy compliance credits.
actions.resultExplain: Meets the letter of the law without cutting your own emissions.

actions.label: Lobby for delay.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money short-term; pollution and reputational cost grow.

justify.question: Why regulate incinerator emissions?
options: To limit toxic gases and particulates harmful to health

roundEvents.title: Retrofit or Retire
roundEvents.brief: An older incinerator is inefficient and dirty
roundEvents.caseFact: Even as incineration plays a central role, cities look to cleaner methods over time.

actions.label: Retrofit with modern tech.
actions.resultExplain: Extends life cleanly and improves recovery.

actions.label: Run it as-is.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap capacity now, dirtier each year.

actions.label: Retire it, shift to recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Cleaner long-term, but loses capacity you must replace.

justify.question: Why move beyond incineration over time?
options: reduction sit higher on the hierarchy and avoid emissions

roundEvents.title: Warmth from Waste
roundEvents.brief: You could pipe the plant's waste heat into a district heating network
roundEvents.caseFact: Copenhagen channels residual-waste heat into its district heating network.

actions.label: Build district heating network.
actions.resultExplain: Maximises useful energy and cuts heating emissions — costly but high-value.

actions.label: Heat public buildings only.
actions.resultExplain: A smaller, safer version of the same idea.

actions.label: Vent the heat.
actions.resultExplain: Wastes recoverable energy.

justify.question: Why is recovering heat "more circular" than venting it?
options: It uses an output as a resource instead of wasting it

roundEvents.title: Credits for Methane
roundEvents.brief: You can earn carbon credits by capturing emissions and methane
roundEvents.caseFact: Waste-sector emissions are large and growing; capturing them has climate and financial value.

actions.label: Methane capture + sell credits.
actions.resultExplain: Monetises emission cuts; reinforces circular behaviour.

actions.label: Capture only, no trading.
actions.resultExplain: Environmental gain without market exposure.

actions.label: Skip it.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; you forgo cuts and revenue.

justify.question: Why does cutting waste-sector emissions matter for climate?
options: Waste emits methane and CO₂ that drive warming

roundEvents.title: Beyond Our Means
roundEvents.brief: Your city's ecological footprint now exceeds local biocapacity — an ecological deficit
roundEvents.caseFact: A region in ecological deficit imports, liquidates its own assets, or emits CO₂ to meet demand.

actions.label: City-wide consumption-reduction strategy.
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root driver (overconsumption); politically tough.

actions.label: Offset via green infrastructure.
actions.resultExplain: Restores some biocapacity locally without cutting demand.

actions.label: Keep importing & emitting.
actions.resultExplain: No change needed now; deficit and damage deepen elsewhere.

justify.question: What does an "ecological deficit" mean?
options: Demand on nature exceeds what local ecosystems can renew

roundEvents.title: Eating the Forest
roundEvents.brief: Urban expansion is pushing into surrounding forest, threatening biodiversity
roundEvents.caseFact: Rio's urban landscape expands over Atlantic Forest; Kalimantan's new capital risks releasing ~48 Mt of carbon.

actions.label: Set a green belt + densify.
actions.resultExplain: Protects habitat and curbs sprawl; limits cheap outward growth.

actions.label: Conservation offsets.
actions.resultExplain: Compensates for some loss without stopping encroachment.

actions.label: Allow expansion.
actions.resultExplain: Fuels growth and revenue; destroys biodiversity and carbon sinks.

justify.question: Why is forest encroachment a waste/footprint issue?
options: Cities absorb resources and land from hinterlands, releasing stored carbon

roundEvents.title: Concrete Hunger
roundEvents.brief: Your building boom demands sand, limestone and metals from fragile ecosystems
roundEvents.caseFact: Extraction of limestone, sand and metals strains threatened ecosystems like karsts, rivers and coasts.

actions.label: Mandate recycled construction materials.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts virgin extraction by reusing demolition waste — technical-cycle circularity.

actions.label: Source-certification for materials.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces the worst impacts without lowering demand.

actions.label: Extract freely.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest build; degrades land and rivers.

justify.question: Why prefer recycled aggregates?
options: They cut virgin extraction and its ecosystem damage

roundEvents.title: Drawing Down the Wells
roundEvents.brief: Growing demand is depleting groundwater faster than it recharges
roundEvents.caseFact: Groundwater from over-harvested aquifers can be seriously depleted by growing urban populations.

actions.label: Water reuse + leak reduction.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the water loop; reduces draw on aquifers.

actions.label: Tariff to curb usage.
actions.resultExplain: Prices the resource; unpopular but effective.

actions.label: Drill more wells.
actions.resultExplain: Meets demand now; accelerates depletion.

justify.question: Why is treating waste-water as a resource circular?
options: Reusing outputs reduces fresh inputs and waste

roundEvents.title: Feeding the City
roundEvents.brief: The city imports food from ever more distant hinterlands, adding emissions and packaging
roundEvents.caseFact: As cities prosper, hinterlands extend from local to regional, national and global scales.

actions.label: Urban farming + organics composting loop.
actions.resultExplain: Returns nutrients to soil and shortens supply chains.

actions.label: Local-sourcing incentives.
actions.resultExplain: Trims the food footprint modestly.

actions.label: Rely on imports.
actions.resultExplain: Convenient; nutrients leave the city as waste, never returned.

justify.question: Why does linear metabolism break natural nutrient cycles?
options: Nutrients are harvested and discarded, not returned to the land

roundEvents.title: The Greenfield Gamble
roundEvents.brief: A proposal to build a satellite city promises growth but huge environmental cost
roundEvents.caseFact: Indonesia's Nusantara echoes Brasília — new capitals risk major deforestation and emissions.

actions.label: Reject; densify the existing city.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids greenfield destruction; harder politically.

actions.label: Build to strict green standards.
actions.resultExplain: Growth with mitigation, but still clears land.

actions.label: Build cheaply and fast.
actions.resultExplain: Maximum growth, maximum ecological harm.

justify.question: Why can a brand-new city be environmentally risky?
options: It clears habitat and locks in new resource demand and emissions

roundEvents.title: Tax the Trash
roundEvents.brief: Pressure builds to tax packaging and disposable goods to cut waste at source
roundEvents.caseFact: Denmark's waste taxes cut landfilled/incinerated waste 26% and lifted recycling to 61%.

actions.label: Strong packaging/disposables tax.
actions.resultExplain: Powerful long-run reducer; immediate political and economic pain.

actions.label: Modest, phased levy.
actions.resultExplain: Gentler, more acceptable, smaller effect.

actions.label: No tax.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids backlash; waste keeps growing.

justify.question: Why does the Polluter-Pays principle work?
options: It creates an economic incentive to reduce and recycle

roundEvents.title: The Climate Ledger
roundEvents.brief: Your waste, transport and energy emissions are climbing on the national ledger
roundEvents.caseFact: Waste-burning emissions (~1.6 Bt CO₂e in 2016) are projected to reach 2.6 Bt by 2050.

actions.label: Decarbonise waste fleet + capture.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts transport and process emissions at the source.

actions.label: Buy carbon offsets.
actions.resultExplain: Neutral on paper; doesn't change your own system.

actions.label: Carry on.
actions.resultExplain: No cost; emissions and climate exposure grow.

justify.question: Why count emissions from the waste sector?
options: Collection, dumping and burning all emit significant GHGs

roundEvents.title: Unwanted Guests
roundEvents.brief: Disturbed land around the city lets invasive species crowd out natives
roundEvents.caseFact: Urbanisation creates disturbed environments where invasive species thrive along roads and edges.

actions.label: Habitat restoration programme.
actions.resultExplain: Rebuilds native ecosystems and resilience.

actions.label: Targeted control only.
actions.resultExplain: Holds the line without restoring habitat.

actions.label: Ignore.
actions.resultExplain: Free; biodiversity erodes further.

justify.question: How does sprawl aid invasive species?
options: Disturbed edge habitats let them outcompete natives

roundEvents.title: Mend, Don't End
roundEvents.brief: A grassroots repair-and-reuse movement wants city backing
roundEvents.caseFact: Reuse cuts the waste stream while letting consumers achieve high waste reduction.

actions.label: Fund a citywide reuse economy.
actions.resultExplain: Builds durable circular culture and local jobs.

actions.label: Support a few flagship repair cafés.
actions.resultExplain: Symbolic momentum, limited scale.

actions.label: Leave it to the market.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; replacement-over-repair culture persists.

justify.question: Where does reuse sit on the waste hierarchy?
options: It is not on the hierarchy

roundEvents.title: The Recyclers Nobody Sees
roundEvents.brief: Thousands of informal pickers already recycle much of your waste — unsafely and unpaid
roundEvents.caseFact: Cairo's ~30,000 zabbaleen recycle a huge share of waste; a pair with a cart serves 350 households a day.

actions.label: Formally integrate them into the system.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap circularity plus dignity, safety and income — a social and environmental win.

actions.label: License but don't support them.
actions.resultExplain: Recognises their role without investing in safety.

actions.label: Evict them.
actions.resultExplain: Removes "informality" but destroys livelihoods and recycling capacity.

justify.question: Why integrate informal recyclers rather than evict them?
options: They already provide cheap recycling; formalising improves safety, equity and circularity

roundEvents.title: The Unserved
roundEvents.brief: Informal settlements have no formal waste service; rubbish piles up
roundEvents.caseFact: Slums often lack any formal waste management, making them hard to render liveable.

actions.label: Community-based collection scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Locally-run service improves health and inclusion affordably.

actions.label: Place communal skips only.
actions.resultExplain: Basic improvement; limited reach and sorting.

actions.label: Leave it unserved.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money; entrenches inequality and disease risk.

justify.question: Why does waste service relate to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)?
options: Liveable, inclusive cities require basic services for all, including slums

roundEvents.title: Vectors
roundEvents.brief: Uncollected, putrefying waste triggers a dengue/cholera outbreak
roundEvents.caseFact: Uncollected waste breeds rats, mosquitoes and flies, spreading cholera, dengue and respiratory illness.

actions.label: Emergency clean-up + vector control.
actions.resultExplain: Stops the outbreak and the breeding grounds; costly but essential.

actions.label: Medical response only.
actions.resultExplain: Treats the sick while the source remains.

actions.label: Downplay it.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest; the epidemic and distrust spread.

justify.question: Why is waste a public-health issue?
options: It creates breeding grounds for disease vectors and contaminates water

roundEvents.title: Collapse at the Dump
roundEvents.brief: A waste mound collapses/catches fire, injuring pickers who live and work there
roundEvents.caseFact: Life expectancy near Deonar is ~39 vs the state's 73.5; a 2016 fire burned for 3 months.

actions.label: Close the dump + relocate and retrain pickers.
actions.resultExplain: Ends the danger and offers dignity; you must replace the lost disposal.

actions.label: Safety measures, keep dump open.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces risk somewhat; the hazard persists.

actions.label: Carry on.
actions.resultExplain: Free; human and environmental cost mounts.

justify.question: Why are open dumps a social-justice issue?
options: The urban poor bear concentrated health and safety harms

roundEvents.title: Lost Schooldays
roundEvents.brief: Children sort waste for family income instead of attending school
roundEvents.caseFact: Manual scavenging often pulls children from education, trapping families in poverty.

actions.label: School-plus-stipend programme.
actions.resultExplain: Breaks the cycle by replacing child income with schooling support.

actions.label: Ban child picking, enforce.
actions.resultExplain: Right in principle, but removes income without alternatives.

actions.label: Ignore.
actions.resultExplain: Free; entrenches intergenerational poverty.

justify.question: How does informal scavenging affect long-term development?
options: It can deny children education, perpetuating poverty

roundEvents.title: Strength in Numbers
roundEvents.brief: Integrated pickers want to form a recycling cooperative with fair prices
roundEvents.caseFact: Scavenged materials feed small industries (e.g., Dar es Salaam's charcoal stoves and buckets).

actions.label: Back the co-op + link to industry.
actions.resultExplain: Turns informal recovery into a stable circular supply chain.

actions.label: Provide equipment only.
actions.resultExplain: Useful boost without the market linkage.

actions.label: Decline.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; misses a low-cost circularity dividend.

justify.question: Why connect recyclers to small industry?
options: Recovered materials become inputs, creating circular local value chains

roundEvents.title: Trash for Tokens
roundEvents.brief: You can let residents trade sorted recyclables for bus tokens or food
roundEvents.caseFact: Curitiba's Green Exchange Programme rewards recycling, boosting participation in low-income areas.

actions.label: Launch a Green Exchange.
actions.resultExplain: High participation and social benefit — recycling people actually want to do.

actions.label: Cash-for-recyclables buy-back.
actions.resultExplain: Effective for some; less community-building than tokens.

actions.label: Mandatory sorting, no reward.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap but resented; participation lags.

justify.question: Why does Curitiba's scheme work so well?
options: It rewards participation and helps low-income residents, so people take part

roundEvents.title: The Women at the Sorting Line
roundEvents.brief: Women and children do much of the hazardous hand-sorting with no protection
roundEvents.caseFact: In Cairo, women and children sort waste at home, facing direct health risks.

actions.label: Provide PPE, clinics and safe facilities.
actions.resultExplain: Protects the most exposed workers; modest cost, real dignity.

actions.label: Health screening only.
actions.resultExplain: Detects harm without preventing exposure.

actions.label: No action.
actions.resultExplain: Free; preventable illness continues.

justify.question: Why focus on who handles waste, not just how much?
options: The social dimension of sustainability includes worker health and equity

roundEvents.title: Cleared Out
roundEvents.brief: Developers lobby to clear a picker settlement for new buildings
roundEvents.caseFact: Authorities increasingly see scavengers as recyclers to incorporate, not remove.

actions.label: Refuse; protect and integrate them.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps recycling capacity and community intact.

actions.label: Relocate with support.
actions.resultExplain: Humane move, but disrupts established recovery networks.

actions.label: Allow eviction.
actions.resultExplain: Quick development gain; recycling and trust collapse.

justify.question: Why might evicting pickers raise total waste costs?
options: You lose free recycling labour and must collect

roundEvents.title: Back to the Soil
roundEvents.brief: Neighbourhoods want to compost food and garden waste locally
roundEvents.caseFact: Organic waste returned to soil is a "biological product" re-entering the organic cycle.

actions.label: Citywide community composting.
actions.resultExplain: Diverts heavy organics, cuts methane, returns nutrients — strongly circular.

actions.label: Central composting facility.
actions.resultExplain: Efficient at scale, less community engagement.

actions.label: Keep organics in landfill.
actions.resultExplain: No effort; organics rot anaerobically and emit methane.

justify.question: Why divert organics from landfill?
options: They emit methane when buried but become useful compost instead

roundEvents.title: The Danish Model
roundEvents.brief: You can enact a comprehensive Polluter-Pays waste tax
roundEvents.caseFact: Denmark taxes landfill (€62.56/t) far above incineration (€6.69/t); it is illegal to landfill what can be incinerated.

actions.label: Full Polluter-Pays law.
actions.resultExplain: Strong, lasting incentive to reduce/recycle; politically costly.

actions.label: Differential disposal tax only.
actions.resultExplain: Nudges waste up the hierarchy without full reform.

actions.label: No new tax.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids backlash; misses the chance to lock in circularity.

justify.question: Why tax landfill more heavily than incineration or recycling?
options: To steer waste up the hierarchy toward less harmful options

roundEvents.title: Aiming for Zero
roundEvents.brief: Commit to a binding zero-waste-to-landfill target like a model city
roundEvents.caseFact: Copenhagen sends <2% to landfill, recycles ~45%, and targets 70% under "Circular Copenhagen."

actions.label: Binding zero-waste roadmap.
actions.resultExplain: A clear circular trajectory; demands sustained investment and participation.

actions.label: Voluntary aspirational target.
actions.resultExplain: Signals intent with weak teeth.

actions.label: Reject as unrealistic.
actions.resultExplain: Pragmatic for a linear city; forgoes the circular prize.

justify.question: What makes a "Zero Waste City" credible?
options: recycle backed by policy and participation

roundEvents.title: Industrial Symbiosis
roundEvents.brief: Factories could share by-products so one firm's waste is another's feedstock
roundEvents.caseFact: "Technical products" can circulate continuously as materials in the industrial cycle.

actions.label: Build an eco-industrial park.
actions.resultExplain: Creates closed technical loops and local value; needs coordination.

actions.label: By-product exchange platform.
actions.resultExplain: Lighter-touch matchmaking for waste-as-feedstock.

actions.label: Status quo.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; industrial materials stay linear.

justify.question: What is industrial symbiosis?
options: Using one process's waste as another's raw material

roundEvents.title: Return and Refund
roundEvents.brief: Enact a national deposit-return law for bottles and cans
roundEvents.caseFact: Deposit-return drives high reuse/recycling rates where collection works.

actions.label: Comprehensive deposit-return law.
actions.resultExplain: Proven, popular, high-capture reuse — strong all-rounder.

actions.label: Pilot in one district.
actions.resultExplain: Tests the model at small scale.

actions.label: Rely on voluntary recycling.
actions.resultExplain: No mandate; capture stays low.

justify.question: Why does a deposit raise return rates?
options: A refundable financial incentive motivates people to return containers

roundEvents.title: Close the Hole
roundEvents.brief: Ban landfilling of recyclable or combustible waste
roundEvents.caseFact: Denmark made it illegal to landfill waste that can be incinerated.

actions.label: Full ban + divert to recycle/recover.
actions.resultExplain: Forces waste up the hierarchy; only works if alternatives exist.

actions.label: Phased ban by material.
actions.resultExplain: Gradual, less disruptive transition.

actions.label: No ban.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps the easy option open; circularity stalls.

justify.question: Why ban landfilling of recyclables/combustibles?
options: It forces higher-value treatment and saves scarce land

roundEvents.title: The Circular Workforce
roundEvents.brief: Invest in training for repair, recycling and remanufacturing jobs
roundEvents.caseFact: Circular activities (repair, recovery, remanufacture) create local employment.

actions.label: Citywide green-skills programme.
actions.resultExplain: Builds the workforce a circular economy needs and lifts liveability.

actions.label: Subsidise circular startups.
actions.resultExplain: Seeds innovation without broad training.

actions.label: No programme.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; the transition lacks skilled labour.

justify.question: Why does a circular economy need workforce investment?

roundEvents.title: Make Makers Pay
roundEvents.brief: Introduce Extended Producer Responsibility so makers fund end-of-life handling
roundEvents.caseFact: Polluter-Pays logic extends to producers funding the disposal of what they sell.

actions.label: Comprehensive EPR scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Shifts cost and design incentives upstream to producers.

actions.label: EPR for packaging & electronics only.
actions.resultExplain: Targets the worst streams; narrower scope.

actions.label: Decline.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids industry friction; the city keeps footing the bill.

justify.question: What does EPR change?
options: Producers bear end-of-life costs, incentivising recyclable design

roundEvents.title: Putting It to the People
roundEvents.brief: A public vote on a waste levy will test your political capital
roundEvents.caseFact: Public attitudes and buy-in are decisive for waste policy success.

actions.label: Campaign transparently for the levy.
actions.resultExplain: Wins durable consent — easier if you've built trust; risky if not.

actions.label: Water down to ensure passage.
actions.resultExplain: A safer, weaker measure that will likely pass.

actions.label: Skip the vote, impose it.
actions.resultExplain: Fast, but deepens distrust and future resistance.

justify.question: Why does public trust shape what policy is possible?
options: Citizens must consent to and participate in waste schemes for them to work

roundEvents.title: Pay As You Throw
roundEvents.brief: Deploy smart bins and pay-as-you-throw pricing
roundEvents.caseFact: Pricing waste by volume creates direct incentives to reduce and sort.

actions.label: Smart metering + PAYT pricing.
actions.resultExplain: Strong per-household incentive to cut waste; needs careful equity design.

actions.label: Smart bins for monitoring only.
actions.resultExplain: Improves logistics without changing behaviour much.

actions.label: No tech.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; misses data-driven gains.

justify.question: Why does pay-as-you-throw cut waste?
options: Charging by volume gives households a direct reason to reduce and sort

roundEvents.title: The Final Audit
roundEvents.brief: A final review weighs whether your city locked in circular or linear habits
roundEvents.caseFact: Optimistic "model" cities adopt circular metabolism; pessimistic ones stay linear and unsustainable.

actions.label: Publish results + commit to continuous improvement.
actions.resultExplain: Cements a circular trajectory and accountability; pays off most for circular cities.

actions.label: Quiet internal review.
actions.resultExplain: Low-key housekeeping with little momentum.

actions.label: Declare success and move on.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids scrutiny; no learning loop, trust dips.

justify.question: What separates a circular "model" city from a linear one?
options: Reducing inputs and recycling outputs back into the system vs treating waste as rubbish

1.title: The Convenience Trap
1.brief: Retailers are wrapping everything in single-use plastic to attract shoppers; bin volumes jump overnight
1.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Levy a packaging charge.
actions.resultExplain: Cutting waste at source is top of the hierarchy, but businesses and shoppers resent paying now; the payoff comes later.

actions.label: Run a "bring-your-own" campaign.
actions.resultExplain: Voluntary behaviour change is cheap and popular but slow and partial.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect it.
actions.resultExplain: Easiest path — but you've locked in linear metabolism and rising waste.

justify.question: Why is reducing packaging "better" than recycling it?
options: It prevents the waste and its disposal cost from ever existing

1.title: Built to be Binned
1.brief: Cheap goods designed to be replaced not repaired are flooding the city
1.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair-and-reuse hubs.
actions.resultExplain: Reuse extends product life and builds a repair culture, but depends on citizen uptake.

actions.label: Right-to-repair regulation.
actions.resultExplain: Forces durability upstream; effective but lobbied against by manufacturers.

actions.label: Expand collection for the extra bulk.
actions.resultExplain: Treats the symptom, not the cause — waste keeps climbing.

justify.question: What does "built-in obsolescence" do to a city's waste stream?
options: Continuously enlarges it as products are replaced not repaired

1.title: Lockdown Leftovers
1.brief: A boom in online food delivery buries the city in takeaway containers
1.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Mandate reusable container scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the loop on packaging, but needs return logistics and buy-in.

actions.label: Add recycling points at hawker centres.
actions.resultExplain: Captures material if residents sort correctly.

actions.label: Absorb it into landfill.
actions.resultExplain: Quick relief, but plastic is non-biodegradable and piles up.

justify.question: Why did delivery culture raise the footprint so fast?
options: More packaging per meal, mostly non-recyclable plastic

1.title: Edge City
1.brief: A new suburb sprawls at the city fringe with no waste collection yet
1.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Extend integrated collection + sorting.
actions.resultExplain: Brings the suburb into a circular system from day one — costly but future-proof.

actions.label: Basic collection only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper, but you've designed in linear habits.

actions.label: Let residents self-manage.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money but invites fly-tipping and resentment.

justify.question: Why design waste systems before a district is built?
options: Retrofitting habits and infrastructure later is far harder and costlier
It is required by law everywhere

1.title: Streets of Refuse
1.brief: Collection can't keep up; rubbish accumulates on streets and catches fire
1.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in collection fleet + routes.
actions.resultExplain: Solves the immediate health risk; doesn't yet address disposal.

actions.label: Partner with informal collectors.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap, boosts circularity and livelihoods — leverages existing networks.

actions.label: Ignore it for now.
actions.resultExplain: Free now, but disease vectors and distrust accumulate.

justify.question: Why is uncollected waste a health emergency, not just an eyesore?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water

1.title: Sort It Out
1.brief: You can launch a citywide source-separation pilot now
1.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full curbside source separation.
actions.resultExplain: Big circularity gain, but expensive and reliant on participation.

actions.label: Drop-off centres only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper, lower capture — convenient for some.

actions.label: Delay until budget improves.
actions.resultExplain: Saves cash; you forgo early circular momentum.

justify.question: Why does source separation only work with citizen participation?
options: Contaminated mixed waste can't be recycled efficiently

1.title: Factory Lines
1.brief: A new industrial park brings jobs — and commercial/industrial waste beyond domestic rubbish
1.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require industrial waste audits + reuse.
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry toward technical-cycle reuse; some firms grumble.

actions.label: Charge firms a disposal fee.
actions.resultExplain: Raises revenue and mild incentive, but firms may cut corners.

actions.label: Accept the waste into general disposal.
actions.resultExplain: Welcomes business but loads your disposal system.

justify.question: Why must cities plan for more than domestic rubbish?
options: Industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied waste streams

1.title: Wrap and Throw
1.brief: With sewers lacking, residents wrap waste and throw it into the river
1.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation + collection.
actions.resultExplain: Tackles root cause; expensive but protects water and health.

actions.label: River clean-up + awareness.
actions.resultExplain: Visible win and behaviour nudge, but waste still needs somewhere to go.

actions.label: Tolerate it.
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now; contaminates wells and waterways downstream.

justify.question: Why does dumping in rivers harm areas far away?
options: Waste and leachate travel downstream and to the sea, spreading contamination

1.title: Teach the Future
1.brief: Schools want a waste-literacy programme to shape habits early
1.caseFact: Changing public attitudes — linking waste to climate and making sorting logical — underpins Copenhagen's success.

actions.label: Citywide schools programme.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap long-term participation builder; slow to show in metrics.

actions.label: One-off media campaign.
actions.resultExplain: A small nudge with limited reach.

actions.label: Skip it.
actions.resultExplain: No cost, no cultural shift toward circularity.

justify.question: Why invest in attitudes, not just bins?

1.title: Digital Discards
1.brief: Rising phone and gadget turnover creates a stream of hazardous electronic waste
1.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back + urban mining.
actions.resultExplain: Recovers critical materials and prevents toxics leaking — "urban mining."

actions.label: Hazardous-waste drop-off only.
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination; recovers less value.

actions.label: Mix it into general waste.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but leaches heavy metals and wastes critical materials.

justify.question: Why treat e-waste as a resource, not rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and toxic substances that must be contained

2.title: The Last Cell
2.brief: Your main landfill will hit capacity within the year
2.caseFact: Cities are running out of landfill space; finding suitable land near dense urban areas is increasingly hard.

actions.label: Build an engineered offshore/lined landfill.
actions.resultExplain: Buys safe capacity (Semakau-style lining) but it's finite and costly.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump.
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief, severe long-term health and fire risk.

actions.label: Slash inflow via recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Attacks the cause, not the hole — slower but sustainable.

justify.question: Why is "just build another dump" a trap?
options: Land is finite and dumps create lasting health

2.title: The Cheap Option
2.brief: Budget hawks push for the cheapest possible disposal
2.caseFact: Over 90% of waste in low-income countries is openly dumped or burned, with serious consequences.

actions.label: Resist; fund engineered disposal.
actions.resultExplain: Protects health, strains budget — the responsible call.

actions.label: Approve the open dump.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money now; mortgages public health later.

actions.label: Hybrid: controlled tipping with cover.
actions.resultExplain: A middle path — better than open dumping, worse than full engineering.

justify.question: What's the core difference between a dump and an engineered landfill?
options: Lining, leachate treatment and controls that limit contamination

2.title: Island of Last Resort
2.brief: Engineers propose a lined offshore landfill with a leachate treatment plant
2.caseFact: Semakau (1999, SGD$610m) is lined with membrane, marine clay and rock; leachate is treated to keep waters clean.

actions.label: Fund it fully.
actions.resultExplain: Best-in-class containment, but heavy upfront debt.

actions.label: Cheaper unlined version.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money but leachate threatens groundwater.

actions.label: Reject; double down on recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids the landfill entirely if you can cut volume enough.

justify.question: Why does lining/leachate treatment matter most when a landfill sits above an aquifer?
options: Leachate can contaminate groundwater used by people

2.title: Not in Our Backyard, Either
2.brief: A rural town that receives your trucked waste refuses further loads
2.caseFact: Collected waste often ends in landfills far from the city, creating "waste hinterlands" that bear the burden.

actions.label: Negotiate benefits-sharing deal.
actions.resultExplain: Fair compensation keeps the route open and respects the hinterland.

actions.label: Truck it further away.
actions.resultExplain: Solves it short-term but raises transport emissions and footprint.

actions.label: Reduce what you send.
actions.resultExplain: Shrinking the stream eases hinterland pressure for good.

justify.question: Why is exporting waste to a hinterland not a real solution?
options: It shifts the environmental burden elsewhere without reducing it

2.title: Poison in the Water
2.brief: Toxic leachate from your site is seeping toward local wells
2.caseFact: Studies link living near landfills to raised risks of birth defects from contaminated water and soil.

actions.label: Install a leachate treatment plant.
actions.resultExplain: Directly stops the contamination pathway; expensive but vital.

actions.label: Provide bottled water, delay fix.
actions.resultExplain: A stopgap that treats symptoms while pollution continues.

actions.label: Deny and monitor only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but erodes trust and worsens the health legacy.

justify.question: What is leachate?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying contaminants

2.title: The Invisible Threat
2.brief: Decomposing organics are building explosive methane pockets in the mound
2.caseFact: Landfills cause ~11% of global methane emissions; methane pockets at Deonar sparked a 3-month fire.

actions.label: Capture methane for energy.
actions.resultExplain: Turns a potent GHG into power — circular and profitable.

actions.label: Flare it off safely.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces explosion risk and converts methane to less-potent CO₂.

actions.label: Leave it.
actions.resultExplain: Free, but fire risk and powerful warming emissions remain.

justify.question: Why is landfill methane a climate priority?
options: Methane is a far more potent greenhouse gas than CO₂ over the short term
It is not produced by landfills

2.title: The Long Haul
2.brief: Hauling waste to distant sites is eating fuel budgets and adding emissions
2.caseFact: Fuel to transport waste over longer distances adds to a city's ecological footprint.

actions.label: Build a local transfer + sorting station.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts haul distance and recovers material en route.

actions.label: Optimise routes with smart logistics.
actions.resultExplain: Efficiency gains without solving the underlying volume.

actions.label: Accept rising costs.
actions.resultExplain: No upfront spend; bleeds budget and emissions each round.

justify.question: Why does hauling distance matter for sustainability?
options: Transport fuel adds emissions and cost to the waste system

2.title: Whose Land?
2.brief: Developers want your landfill buffer land for housing; residents want a park
2.caseFact: Proximity of landfills to residential areas creates land-use conflicts in dense cities.

actions.label: Convert a closed cell into a park.
actions.resultExplain: Well-capped landfills can become playing fields — a liveability win.

actions.label: Sell to developers.
actions.resultExplain: Quick revenue, but builds homes near contamination and angers residents.

actions.label: Keep as buffer.
actions.resultExplain: Safe and cautious; forgoes both revenue and amenity.

justify.question: How can old landfill land be reused safely?
options: capped sites can host parks or playing fields

2.title: Fly-Tipping
2.brief: New disposal fees push some to dump waste illegally on roadsides and lots
2.caseFact: Where formal disposal is costly or lacking, illegal dumping becomes an "inevitable" reality.

actions.label: Free drop-off + enforcement.
actions.resultExplain: Removes the incentive to dump while penalising offenders.

actions.label: Heavy fines only.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap deterrent, but drives dumping underground if no easy alternative.

actions.label: Ignore it.
actions.resultExplain: Free, but blight and contamination spread.

justify.question: Why can disposal fees backfire without easy alternatives?
options: People resort to illegal dumping when legal disposal is too costly or hard

2.title: Digging Up the Past
2.brief: An old landfill could be mined to reclaim land and recover materials
2.caseFact: Reclaiming and reusing old sites extends land value and recovers recyclables (urban mining).

actions.label: Landfill mining + remediation.
actions.resultExplain: Recovers space and materials, but it's costly and disruptive.

actions.label: Cap and re-green only.
actions.resultExplain: Lower cost amenity gain without reclaiming materials.

actions.label: Leave the old site.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; land and materials stay locked away.

justify.question: What is "urban mining"?

3.title: Burn or Bury?
3.brief: A modern incinerator could slash landfill volume and generate power
3.caseFact: Incineration can cut waste volume 80–90%; Singapore's plants supply 2–3% of national electricity.

actions.label: Build a modern incinerator.
actions.resultExplain: Massive capacity + energy income, but pollution, NIMBY and heavy debt.

actions.label: Smaller waste-to-energy plant.
actions.resultExplain: Lower impact and cost; less capacity relief.

actions.label: Avoid burning; expand recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps air clean and materials in the loop, but capacity relief is slower.

justify.question: What is the central trade-off of incineration?
options: It frees land and makes energy but emits pollutants and faces NIMBY

3.title: Not Near My Home
3.brief: Residents near the proposed/active plant protest over health and property values
3.caseFact: Incinerators raise NIMBY concerns; studies link downwind living to health effects and lower property prices.

actions.label: Transparent siting + health monitoring.
actions.resultExplain: Engagement and monitoring rebuild trust; slows the project.

actions.label: Compensate affected residents.
actions.resultExplain: Buys acceptance without addressing emissions.

actions.label: Override the protest.
actions.resultExplain: Fast, but poisons public trust for every future policy.

justify.question: Why does public trust matter for waste infrastructure?
options: Without buy-in, siting and participation in future schemes get much harder

3.title: Private Power
3.brief: A private firm offers to build and run a waste-to-energy plant
3.caseFact: Edmonton's incinerator handles waste from seven London boroughs, generating 55 MW for ~24,000 homes.

actions.label: Public-private partnership.
actions.resultExplain: Shares cost and risk; you cede some control over emissions standards.

actions.label: Public build, public control.
actions.resultExplain: Full control of standards, full cost and debt.

actions.label: Decline; stay landfill+recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids burning and debt; capacity pressure persists.

justify.question: A risk of privatised waste-to-energy?

3.title: The Downwind Data
3.brief: A study finds elevated health risks downwind of your incinerator
3.caseFact: Research associated Edmonton's incinerator with downwind stillbirths far above upwind rates.

actions.label: Retrofit advanced flue-gas scrubbers.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts dioxins/particulates sharply; costly but protects health.

actions.label: Adjust operating hours/wind.
actions.resultExplain: Marginal mitigation; the core emissions remain.

actions.label: Dispute the study.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, but harms residents and credibility.

justify.question: Why is incineration "waste of energy" sometimes, not "waste to energy"?
options: Emissions and health costs can outweigh the energy benefit if uncontrolled

3.title: What's Left Behind
3.brief: Incineration leaves toxic bottom and fly ash that must go somewhere
3.caseFact: Incineration converts waste to ash, flue gas and heat — ash disposal is a real challenge.

actions.label: Treat ash + reuse in construction.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the loop on residue where safe; needs careful testing.

actions.label: Send ash to a lined cell.
actions.resultExplain: Safe containment, consumes precious landfill space.

actions.label: Dump ash untreated.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest; heavy metals contaminate land and water.

justify.question: Why doesn't incineration make waste "disappear"?
options: It leaves ash and emissions that still need management

3.title: Selling the Spark
3.brief: The grid offers a contract for your plant's electricity
3.caseFact: Waste-to-energy can offset costs — Singapore's plants supply 2–3% of the grid.

actions.label: Long-term grid contract + heat reuse.
actions.resultExplain: Locks in revenue and recovers heat — best value from burning.

actions.label: Spot-market sales.
actions.resultExplain: Flexible income, exposed to price swings.

actions.label: Power your own facilities only.
actions.resultExplain: Modest savings, no external revenue.

justify.question: How does energy recovery change incineration's balance?
options: Revenue and displaced fossil energy partly offset its costs

3.title: The New Rules
3.brief: National regulators impose stricter air-quality limits
3.caseFact: Burning waste releases NOx, CO₂, SO₂, dioxins and particulates — regulation targets these.

actions.label: Upgrade to comply early.
actions.resultExplain: Future-proofs operations and protects health; upfront cost.

actions.label: Buy compliance credits.
actions.resultExplain: Meets the letter of the law without cutting your own emissions.

actions.label: Lobby for delay.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money short-term; pollution and reputational cost grow.

justify.question: Why regulate incinerator emissions?
options: To limit toxic gases and particulates harmful to health

3.title: Retrofit or Retire
3.brief: An older incinerator is inefficient and dirty
3.caseFact: Even as incineration plays a central role, cities look to cleaner methods over time.

actions.label: Retrofit with modern tech.
actions.resultExplain: Extends life cleanly and improves recovery.

actions.label: Run it as-is.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap capacity now, dirtier each year.

actions.label: Retire it, shift to recycling.
actions.resultExplain: Cleaner long-term, but loses capacity you must replace.

justify.question: Why move beyond incineration over time?
options: reduction sit higher on the hierarchy and avoid emissions

3.title: Warmth from Waste
3.brief: You could pipe the plant's waste heat into a district heating network
3.caseFact: Copenhagen channels residual-waste heat into its district heating network.

actions.label: Build district heating network.
actions.resultExplain: Maximises useful energy and cuts heating emissions — costly but high-value.

actions.label: Heat public buildings only.
actions.resultExplain: A smaller, safer version of the same idea.

actions.label: Vent the heat.
actions.resultExplain: Wastes recoverable energy.

justify.question: Why is recovering heat "more circular" than venting it?
options: It uses an output as a resource instead of wasting it

3.title: Credits for Methane
3.brief: You can earn carbon credits by capturing emissions and methane
3.caseFact: Waste-sector emissions are large and growing; capturing them has climate and financial value.

actions.label: Methane capture + sell credits.
actions.resultExplain: Monetises emission cuts; reinforces circular behaviour.

actions.label: Capture only, no trading.
actions.resultExplain: Environmental gain without market exposure.

actions.label: Skip it.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; you forgo cuts and revenue.

justify.question: Why does cutting waste-sector emissions matter for climate?
options: Waste emits methane and CO₂ that drive warming

4.title: Beyond Our Means
4.brief: Your city's ecological footprint now exceeds local biocapacity — an ecological deficit
4.caseFact: A region in ecological deficit imports, liquidates its own assets, or emits CO₂ to meet demand.

actions.label: City-wide consumption-reduction strategy.
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root driver (overconsumption); politically tough.

actions.label: Offset via green infrastructure.
actions.resultExplain: Restores some biocapacity locally without cutting demand.

actions.label: Keep importing & emitting.
actions.resultExplain: No change needed now; deficit and damage deepen elsewhere.

justify.question: What does an "ecological deficit" mean?
options: Demand on nature exceeds what local ecosystems can renew

4.title: Eating the Forest
4.brief: Urban expansion is pushing into surrounding forest, threatening biodiversity
4.caseFact: Rio's urban landscape expands over Atlantic Forest; Kalimantan's new capital risks releasing ~48 Mt of carbon.

actions.label: Set a green belt + densify.
actions.resultExplain: Protects habitat and curbs sprawl; limits cheap outward growth.

actions.label: Conservation offsets.
actions.resultExplain: Compensates for some loss without stopping encroachment.

actions.label: Allow expansion.
actions.resultExplain: Fuels growth and revenue; destroys biodiversity and carbon sinks.

justify.question: Why is forest encroachment a waste/footprint issue?
options: Cities absorb resources and land from hinterlands, releasing stored carbon

4.title: Concrete Hunger
4.brief: Your building boom demands sand, limestone and metals from fragile ecosystems
4.caseFact: Extraction of limestone, sand and metals strains threatened ecosystems like karsts, rivers and coasts.

actions.label: Mandate recycled construction materials.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts virgin extraction by reusing demolition waste — technical-cycle circularity.

actions.label: Source-certification for materials.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces the worst impacts without lowering demand.

actions.label: Extract freely.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest build; degrades land and rivers.

justify.question: Why prefer recycled aggregates?
options: They cut virgin extraction and its ecosystem damage

4.title: Drawing Down the Wells
4.brief: Growing demand is depleting groundwater faster than it recharges
4.caseFact: Groundwater from over-harvested aquifers can be seriously depleted by growing urban populations.

actions.label: Water reuse + leak reduction.
actions.resultExplain: Closes the water loop; reduces draw on aquifers.

actions.label: Tariff to curb usage.
actions.resultExplain: Prices the resource; unpopular but effective.

actions.label: Drill more wells.
actions.resultExplain: Meets demand now; accelerates depletion.

justify.question: Why is treating waste-water as a resource circular?
options: Reusing outputs reduces fresh inputs and waste

4.title: Feeding the City
4.brief: The city imports food from ever more distant hinterlands, adding emissions and packaging
4.caseFact: As cities prosper, hinterlands extend from local to regional, national and global scales.

actions.label: Urban farming + organics composting loop.
actions.resultExplain: Returns nutrients to soil and shortens supply chains.

actions.label: Local-sourcing incentives.
actions.resultExplain: Trims the food footprint modestly.

actions.label: Rely on imports.
actions.resultExplain: Convenient; nutrients leave the city as waste, never returned.

justify.question: Why does linear metabolism break natural nutrient cycles?
options: Nutrients are harvested and discarded, not returned to the land

4.title: The Greenfield Gamble
4.brief: A proposal to build a satellite city promises growth but huge environmental cost
4.caseFact: Indonesia's Nusantara echoes Brasília — new capitals risk major deforestation and emissions.

actions.label: Reject; densify the existing city.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids greenfield destruction; harder politically.

actions.label: Build to strict green standards.
actions.resultExplain: Growth with mitigation, but still clears land.

actions.label: Build cheaply and fast.
actions.resultExplain: Maximum growth, maximum ecological harm.

justify.question: Why can a brand-new city be environmentally risky?
options: It clears habitat and locks in new resource demand and emissions

4.title: Tax the Trash
4.brief: Pressure builds to tax packaging and disposable goods to cut waste at source
4.caseFact: Denmark's waste taxes cut landfilled/incinerated waste 26% and lifted recycling to 61%.

actions.label: Strong packaging/disposables tax.
actions.resultExplain: Powerful long-run reducer; immediate political and economic pain.

actions.label: Modest, phased levy.
actions.resultExplain: Gentler, more acceptable, smaller effect.

actions.label: No tax.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids backlash; waste keeps growing.

justify.question: Why does the Polluter-Pays principle work?
options: It creates an economic incentive to reduce and recycle

4.title: The Climate Ledger
4.brief: Your waste, transport and energy emissions are climbing on the national ledger
4.caseFact: Waste-burning emissions (~1.6 Bt CO₂e in 2016) are projected to reach 2.6 Bt by 2050.

actions.label: Decarbonise waste fleet + capture.
actions.resultExplain: Cuts transport and process emissions at the source.

actions.label: Buy carbon offsets.
actions.resultExplain: Neutral on paper; doesn't change your own system.

actions.label: Carry on.
actions.resultExplain: No cost; emissions and climate exposure grow.

justify.question: Why count emissions from the waste sector?
options: Collection, dumping and burning all emit significant GHGs

4.title: Unwanted Guests
4.brief: Disturbed land around the city lets invasive species crowd out natives
4.caseFact: Urbanisation creates disturbed environments where invasive species thrive along roads and edges.

actions.label: Habitat restoration programme.
actions.resultExplain: Rebuilds native ecosystems and resilience.

actions.label: Targeted control only.
actions.resultExplain: Holds the line without restoring habitat.

actions.label: Ignore.
actions.resultExplain: Free; biodiversity erodes further.

justify.question: How does sprawl aid invasive species?
options: Disturbed edge habitats let them outcompete natives

4.title: Mend, Don't End
4.brief: A grassroots repair-and-reuse movement wants city backing
4.caseFact: Reuse cuts the waste stream while letting consumers achieve high waste reduction.

actions.label: Fund a citywide reuse economy.
actions.resultExplain: Builds durable circular culture and local jobs.

actions.label: Support a few flagship repair cafés.
actions.resultExplain: Symbolic momentum, limited scale.

actions.label: Leave it to the market.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; replacement-over-repair culture persists.

justify.question: Where does reuse sit on the waste hierarchy?
options: It is not on the hierarchy

5.title: The Recyclers Nobody Sees
5.brief: Thousands of informal pickers already recycle much of your waste — unsafely and unpaid
5.caseFact: Cairo's ~30,000 zabbaleen recycle a huge share of waste; a pair with a cart serves 350 households a day.

actions.label: Formally integrate them into the system.
actions.resultExplain: Cheap circularity plus dignity, safety and income — a social and environmental win.

actions.label: License but don't support them.
actions.resultExplain: Recognises their role without investing in safety.

actions.label: Evict them.
actions.resultExplain: Removes "informality" but destroys livelihoods and recycling capacity.

justify.question: Why integrate informal recyclers rather than evict them?
options: They already provide cheap recycling; formalising improves safety, equity and circularity

5.title: The Unserved
5.brief: Informal settlements have no formal waste service; rubbish piles up
5.caseFact: Slums often lack any formal waste management, making them hard to render liveable.

actions.label: Community-based collection scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Locally-run service improves health and inclusion affordably.

actions.label: Place communal skips only.
actions.resultExplain: Basic improvement; limited reach and sorting.

actions.label: Leave it unserved.
actions.resultExplain: Saves money; entrenches inequality and disease risk.

justify.question: Why does waste service relate to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)?
options: Liveable, inclusive cities require basic services for all, including slums

5.title: Vectors
5.brief: Uncollected, putrefying waste triggers a dengue/cholera outbreak
5.caseFact: Uncollected waste breeds rats, mosquitoes and flies, spreading cholera, dengue and respiratory illness.

actions.label: Emergency clean-up + vector control.
actions.resultExplain: Stops the outbreak and the breeding grounds; costly but essential.

actions.label: Medical response only.
actions.resultExplain: Treats the sick while the source remains.

actions.label: Downplay it.
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest; the epidemic and distrust spread.

justify.question: Why is waste a public-health issue?
options: It creates breeding grounds for disease vectors and contaminates water

5.title: Collapse at the Dump
5.brief: A waste mound collapses/catches fire, injuring pickers who live and work there
5.caseFact: Life expectancy near Deonar is ~39 vs the state's 73.5; a 2016 fire burned for 3 months.

actions.label: Close the dump + relocate and retrain pickers.
actions.resultExplain: Ends the danger and offers dignity; you must replace the lost disposal.

actions.label: Safety measures, keep dump open.
actions.resultExplain: Reduces risk somewhat; the hazard persists.

actions.label: Carry on.
actions.resultExplain: Free; human and environmental cost mounts.

justify.question: Why are open dumps a social-justice issue?
options: The urban poor bear concentrated health and safety harms

5.title: Lost Schooldays
5.brief: Children sort waste for family income instead of attending school
5.caseFact: Manual scavenging often pulls children from education, trapping families in poverty.

actions.label: School-plus-stipend programme.
actions.resultExplain: Breaks the cycle by replacing child income with schooling support.

actions.label: Ban child picking, enforce.
actions.resultExplain: Right in principle, but removes income without alternatives.

actions.label: Ignore.
actions.resultExplain: Free; entrenches intergenerational poverty.

justify.question: How does informal scavenging affect long-term development?
options: It can deny children education, perpetuating poverty

5.title: Strength in Numbers
5.brief: Integrated pickers want to form a recycling cooperative with fair prices
5.caseFact: Scavenged materials feed small industries (e.g., Dar es Salaam's charcoal stoves and buckets).

actions.label: Back the co-op + link to industry.
actions.resultExplain: Turns informal recovery into a stable circular supply chain.

actions.label: Provide equipment only.
actions.resultExplain: Useful boost without the market linkage.

actions.label: Decline.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; misses a low-cost circularity dividend.

justify.question: Why connect recyclers to small industry?
options: Recovered materials become inputs, creating circular local value chains

5.title: Trash for Tokens
5.brief: You can let residents trade sorted recyclables for bus tokens or food
5.caseFact: Curitiba's Green Exchange Programme rewards recycling, boosting participation in low-income areas.

actions.label: Launch a Green Exchange.
actions.resultExplain: High participation and social benefit — recycling people actually want to do.

actions.label: Cash-for-recyclables buy-back.
actions.resultExplain: Effective for some; less community-building than tokens.

actions.label: Mandatory sorting, no reward.
