actions.resultExplain: Cheap but resented; participation lags.

justify.question: Why does Curitiba's scheme work so well?
options: It rewards participation and helps low-income residents, so people take part

5.title: The Women at the Sorting Line
5.brief: Women and children do much of the hazardous hand-sorting with no protection
5.caseFact: In Cairo, women and children sort waste at home, facing direct health risks.

actions.label: Provide PPE, clinics and safe facilities.
actions.resultExplain: Protects the most exposed workers; modest cost, real dignity.

actions.label: Health screening only.
actions.resultExplain: Detects harm without preventing exposure.

actions.label: No action.
actions.resultExplain: Free; preventable illness continues.

justify.question: Why focus on who handles waste, not just how much?
options: The social dimension of sustainability includes worker health and equity

5.title: Cleared Out
5.brief: Developers lobby to clear a picker settlement for new buildings
5.caseFact: Authorities increasingly see scavengers as recyclers to incorporate, not remove.

actions.label: Refuse; protect and integrate them.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps recycling capacity and community intact.

actions.label: Relocate with support.
actions.resultExplain: Humane move, but disrupts established recovery networks.

actions.label: Allow eviction.
actions.resultExplain: Quick development gain; recycling and trust collapse.

justify.question: Why might evicting pickers raise total waste costs?
options: You lose free recycling labour and must collect

5.title: Back to the Soil
5.brief: Neighbourhoods want to compost food and garden waste locally
5.caseFact: Organic waste returned to soil is a "biological product" re-entering the organic cycle.

actions.label: Citywide community composting.
actions.resultExplain: Diverts heavy organics, cuts methane, returns nutrients — strongly circular.

actions.label: Central composting facility.
actions.resultExplain: Efficient at scale, less community engagement.

actions.label: Keep organics in landfill.
actions.resultExplain: No effort; organics rot anaerobically and emit methane.

justify.question: Why divert organics from landfill?
options: They emit methane when buried but become useful compost instead

6.title: The Danish Model
6.brief: You can enact a comprehensive Polluter-Pays waste tax
6.caseFact: Denmark taxes landfill (€62.56/t) far above incineration (€6.69/t); it is illegal to landfill what can be incinerated.

actions.label: Full Polluter-Pays law.
actions.resultExplain: Strong, lasting incentive to reduce/recycle; politically costly.

actions.label: Differential disposal tax only.
actions.resultExplain: Nudges waste up the hierarchy without full reform.

actions.label: No new tax.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids backlash; misses the chance to lock in circularity.

justify.question: Why tax landfill more heavily than incineration or recycling?
options: To steer waste up the hierarchy toward less harmful options

6.title: Aiming for Zero
6.brief: Commit to a binding zero-waste-to-landfill target like a model city
6.caseFact: Copenhagen sends <2% to landfill, recycles ~45%, and targets 70% under "Circular Copenhagen."

actions.label: Binding zero-waste roadmap.
actions.resultExplain: A clear circular trajectory; demands sustained investment and participation.

actions.label: Voluntary aspirational target.
actions.resultExplain: Signals intent with weak teeth.

actions.label: Reject as unrealistic.
actions.resultExplain: Pragmatic for a linear city; forgoes the circular prize.

justify.question: What makes a "Zero Waste City" credible?
options: recycle backed by policy and participation

6.title: Industrial Symbiosis
6.brief: Factories could share by-products so one firm's waste is another's feedstock
6.caseFact: "Technical products" can circulate continuously as materials in the industrial cycle.

actions.label: Build an eco-industrial park.
actions.resultExplain: Creates closed technical loops and local value; needs coordination.

actions.label: By-product exchange platform.
actions.resultExplain: Lighter-touch matchmaking for waste-as-feedstock.

actions.label: Status quo.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; industrial materials stay linear.

justify.question: What is industrial symbiosis?
options: Using one process's waste as another's raw material

6.title: Return and Refund
6.brief: Enact a national deposit-return law for bottles and cans
6.caseFact: Deposit-return drives high reuse/recycling rates where collection works.

actions.label: Comprehensive deposit-return law.
actions.resultExplain: Proven, popular, high-capture reuse — strong all-rounder.

actions.label: Pilot in one district.
actions.resultExplain: Tests the model at small scale.

actions.label: Rely on voluntary recycling.
actions.resultExplain: No mandate; capture stays low.

justify.question: Why does a deposit raise return rates?
options: A refundable financial incentive motivates people to return containers

6.title: Close the Hole
6.brief: Ban landfilling of recyclable or combustible waste
6.caseFact: Denmark made it illegal to landfill waste that can be incinerated.

actions.label: Full ban + divert to recycle/recover.
actions.resultExplain: Forces waste up the hierarchy; only works if alternatives exist.

actions.label: Phased ban by material.
actions.resultExplain: Gradual, less disruptive transition.

actions.label: No ban.
actions.resultExplain: Keeps the easy option open; circularity stalls.

justify.question: Why ban landfilling of recyclables/combustibles?
options: It forces higher-value treatment and saves scarce land

6.title: The Circular Workforce
6.brief: Invest in training for repair, recycling and remanufacturing jobs
6.caseFact: Circular activities (repair, recovery, remanufacture) create local employment.

actions.label: Citywide green-skills programme.
actions.resultExplain: Builds the workforce a circular economy needs and lifts liveability.

actions.label: Subsidise circular startups.
actions.resultExplain: Seeds innovation without broad training.

actions.label: No programme.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; the transition lacks skilled labour.

justify.question: Why does a circular economy need workforce investment?

6.title: Make Makers Pay
6.brief: Introduce Extended Producer Responsibility so makers fund end-of-life handling
6.caseFact: Polluter-Pays logic extends to producers funding the disposal of what they sell.

actions.label: Comprehensive EPR scheme.
actions.resultExplain: Shifts cost and design incentives upstream to producers.

actions.label: EPR for packaging & electronics only.
actions.resultExplain: Targets the worst streams; narrower scope.

actions.label: Decline.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids industry friction; the city keeps footing the bill.

justify.question: What does EPR change?
options: Producers bear end-of-life costs, incentivising recyclable design

6.title: Putting It to the People
6.brief: A public vote on a waste levy will test your political capital
6.caseFact: Public attitudes and buy-in are decisive for waste policy success.

actions.label: Campaign transparently for the levy.
actions.resultExplain: Wins durable consent — easier if you've built trust; risky if not.

actions.label: Water down to ensure passage.
actions.resultExplain: A safer, weaker measure that will likely pass.

actions.label: Skip the vote, impose it.
actions.resultExplain: Fast, but deepens distrust and future resistance.

justify.question: Why does public trust shape what policy is possible?
options: Citizens must consent to and participate in waste schemes for them to work

6.title: Pay As You Throw
6.brief: Deploy smart bins and pay-as-you-throw pricing
6.caseFact: Pricing waste by volume creates direct incentives to reduce and sort.

actions.label: Smart metering + PAYT pricing.
actions.resultExplain: Strong per-household incentive to cut waste; needs careful equity design.

actions.label: Smart bins for monitoring only.
actions.resultExplain: Improves logistics without changing behaviour much.

actions.label: No tech.
actions.resultExplain: No spend; misses data-driven gains.

justify.question: Why does pay-as-you-throw cut waste?
options: Charging by volume gives households a direct reason to reduce and sort

6.title: The Final Audit
6.brief: A final review weighs whether your city locked in circular or linear habits
6.caseFact: Optimistic "model" cities adopt circular metabolism; pessimistic ones stay linear and unsustainable.

actions.label: Publish results + commit to continuous improvement.
actions.resultExplain: Cements a circular trajectory and accountability; pays off most for circular cities.

actions.label: Quiet internal review.
actions.resultExplain: Low-key housekeeping with little momentum.

actions.label: Declare success and move on.
actions.resultExplain: Avoids scrutiny; no learning loop, trust dips.

justify.question: What separates a circular "model" city from a linear one?
options: Reducing inputs and recycling outputs back into the system vs treating waste as rubbish
